author: @ViaExplore
text:
0:35 46g total weight 10cm x 10cm x 0.6cm = V = 60cm3
0:45 30mWh/g of finished device (which means that his cell should have 30mWh/g x 46g = 1.38Wh)
0:52 14.4V cell
1:04 peak charge current 6A (initial power = 14.4V x 6A = 86.4W, Internal resistance R = 14.4V/6A = 2.4ohm)
1:23 discharge test 30mA for 6~6.5h ~= 30mA x 6.5h = 195mAh (from 14.4V to 0V)

Assuming linear voltage drop on constant current discharge, this give us (14.4V x 195mAh)/2 = 1.4Wh
By weight this cell has 1.4Wh x 1000g / 46g = 30Wh/kg (lead acid battery has ~34Wh/kg)
By volume this cell has 1.4Wh / 60cm3 = 23mWh/cm3 (lead acid battery has 100mWh/cm3)
Energy per inner plates 1.4Wh/6 = 233mWh. By surface area it has 233mWh / 100cm2 = 2.33mWh/cm2 (this is remarkable in all carbon cells!).

So, it is almost at the level of the lead acid battery, but with much higher charge/discharge life cycle. 
Nice work Robert, keep it up!

PS: One hint, take those carbon plates, put first LiCl in between, thread them at 3.6-4V for 2h (it will gas and intercalate). 
Then wash out completely the LiCl and use those plates now in your configuration. 
Let us know the results (in my case this extended the surface area by factor of ~10).

author: @jabosk3045
text: In the testing you should also include abuse like short circuits, puncture tests, extreme hot and cold, ect. I wonder how it'll hold up to LCBs (lithium ceramic batteries)

replies.author: @robm6510
replies.text: I'm wondering this also.  They look great as lab prototypes ... But these things need protection.  They're gonna explode alot more violently than a lithium, and lithiums can be fairly violent as is.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no they won't explode - the reason lithium explodes is because the electrolyte is basically petrol. This is water

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It is basically an all carbon water based device with around the energy density of lead acid. So, for safety and cheapness it'll knock it out of the park but for energy denisty it won't - that's about it

replies.author: @robm6510
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering No discharging between parts?  Electrical arc is what I'm referring to as an explosion ... Not your materials themselves.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: if you connect the terminals carelessly it is going to spark - yes - all high power batteries do that. Beyond that - why would it explode?

author: @Duugstep
text:
What effects how many amps a battery or cap can hold?? Because for grid applications all the batteries are single cell (2 volts approx) but hold a crazy amount of amps like 100amps and then loads are just wired together to get the required voltage. 
They are open lead acid batteries and not much bigger that a pack of A4 paper

Apologies for the dumb question but I'm no genius battery technology just fascinates me

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: ampage is determined by mass of active material and the ability of the active material to store as an intrinsic property.

author: @robm6510
text: Robert, have you experimented with trying to make this carbon ink in a decent spin-on weight?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: what's a 'spin-on weight' mate?

replies.author: @robm6510
replies.text:
Suspended in fluid that can be spun onto a substrate and get reliable coverage.  I think it will depend on what grain size the carbon particles are that you have in these inks that will determine how thin you could put it on and not have pits/voids.  The more defect free that coating, the more efficient it will be and the lower the number of wraps you'll use for the same output.  I'm just picturing trying to get an automatted coat tool to make these (or even doing it myself if I could sneak some of this into the office 😁).  You're painting them on, have you measured the thickness of that paint layer?  Or looked at the defects in it as far as the coat.

I do admire what you're doing.  It makes me want to drop some dough and start a garage business of my own.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: spin coating doesn't seem an good method for mass production to me mate - i would be much more interested in silk screen, gravure or doctor blade - wouldn't you? There percent solids content and wet film thickness would be key parameters for quality control - in which case you could just weigh them as the do in current lithium battery production

replies.author: @robm6510
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Silk screen, again perhaps for cost.  I think we are looking at it being able to be applied across the entire path that the rest of the electronics industry took.  Just skipping some generations.  It's easier to jump ahead now that there are already alot of facilities tooled to run this stuff.  You don't have to break as much new ground if you just adapt the ink to drop into current cutting edge processes.

replies.author: @robm6510
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Also I don't like the idea of relying heavily on weight (no pun intended).  I'd much rather control to plate thicknesses, precise surface areas of those plates (photo defined?), conductivity, per layer capacitance.  Weight alone leaves alot of unknowns and variability.

replies.author: @claymccormick1203
replies.text: there is a hole subculture devoted too making things from recycled plastic this one took 15 seconds to find there are many others and heaters and controllers are cheap on amazon Ali express or eBay.  https://youtu.be/wPyZ1g2M9qI something like this can easily be made.this is a slick injection molder https://youtu.be/_gJ0PDWs0iU though you can go with a simpler or more complex design as to do what you need my thought is to use a vertical piece of heated pipe with a lever to  do the injecting controllers are cheap and it would not be either hard or time consuming to build. and if you let people know you can get all the milk jugs you can use until you scale up to a commercial size then you just buy plastic.the mold would need much machining.

author: @michaelinamoment
text:
I am thinking about getting some graphene powder from the shop for an experiment. If mixed with distilled water do you know what the conductive properties of the resulting fluid would be?
I want to try making a Leyden Jar with this approach!

replies.author: @robm6510
replies.text: Good idea, though keeping it homogenized is the hard part.  This stuff seems like a slurry of sorts.  You'd need another agent in there (which will likely cut down on conductivity a bit) or keep it mixing somehow.

replies.author: @michaelinamoment
replies.text: Thats actually very interesting to think about in regards to what happens to charge afterwards. You could probably shake it right up and will just be fine :D As long as the charge does not ground it should remain on the dielectric.

replies.author: @robm6510
replies.text:
@michaelinamoment They would dissapate though, right?  Essentially you'd lose all your stored charge over time as the pieces touched one another and collected at the bottom with no dielectric to create a difference in potential between them or they'd likely cling on the anode/cathode you have charging it, like an electroplating bath.  Some combination of both.
I like the idea of trying to control the interface more as he does in building caps.  There is an intrinsic benefit to having a high almost battery like density, but you still retain a hard connection to the cathode and anodes.  Quick charging/discharging.

replies.author: @michaelinamoment
replies.text: @robm6510 fun fact charge does not collect on the cathode and anode. the cathode and anode or a means to deliver charge it to the dielectric which then separate the charge. If you think about it in terms of electrons trying to get to positive charged particles. It becomes easier to imagine. You can charge capacitor take it apart touch the metal together and would not ground a charge. but then put it back together and Bridge the two sides and you would get a discharge :)

replies.author: @Web3Dre
replies.text: Sorry to chime in. But I think you want exfoliated carbon if you are thinking of increasing the amount of energy you can store in the jar.

author: @Web3Dre

author: @aga5897
text:
Some carbon in a CD Case. 
Hmm.
Well, re-using an existing mass-produced (therefore cheap) format is Smart.
You are actually onto something you know ?
Something Enormous.

replies.author: @stemcampprojects7951
replies.text: While it could make a lot of sense to use a CD case, I don't think that's what they are doing.  The size Rob quoted in the video is just a bit smaller than a CD case.  I could be wrong, as usual. <grin>
